questions.q: What is the primary feature of a wireless mesh network?
questions.exp: Wireless mesh networks use a decentralized architecture — each node can route traffic, so there is no single point of failure.

questions.q: Which characteristic is NOT a feature of wireless mesh networking?
questions.exp: Wireless mesh networks are decentralized by nature. Centralized architecture is the opposite of what mesh networking offers.

questions.q: In the context of mesh networks, what does the term 'self-healing' refer to?
opts: The ability to manually repair physical damage
Healing of hardware issues without human intervention
The network's ability to automatically reroute data if a node fails
The process of adding new nodes to the network
questions.exp: Self-healing means the network can automatically detect a failed node and reroute data through alternative paths without human intervention.

questions.q: Which is NOT a challenge mentioned for wireless mesh networks?
questions.exp: Unlimited bandwidth is not a challenge — it is not a realistic characteristic of mesh networks. Real challenges include bandwidth allocation, interference, and synchronization.

questions.q: What is a typical method to prevent malicious nodes from joining a wireless mesh network?
questions.exp: Authentication ensures that only trusted, verified nodes can join the mesh network, preventing malicious nodes from gaining access.

questions.q: In a distributed mesh network, how are routes between nodes established?
opts: All communication goes through a master node
Nodes communicate to determine adjacency and routes
questions.exp: In a distributed mesh network, nodes exchange routing information with each other directly to discover neighbours and establish routes — no central authority is needed.

questions.q: What does Adaptive Flooding adjust based on?
questions.exp: Adaptive Flooding dynamically adjusts its behaviour based on current network conditions and available resources to optimise message propagation.

questions.q: Which flooding algorithm divides the network into subnets for routing?
questions.exp: Reduced Flooding divides the network into subnets, limiting the scope of flood messages to reduce overhead.

questions.q: What is a disadvantage of simple flooding in mesh networks?
opts: It is too simple to implement
It generates a large number of redundant messages
questions.exp: Simple flooding broadcasts packets to every neighbour, causing a broadcast storm — a large number of redundant messages that waste bandwidth and energy.

questions.q: Dynamic Source Routing (DSR) has multiple routes per destination.
questions.exp: True — DSR maintains multiple cached routes to each destination, allowing it to quickly switch to an alternative if the primary route fails.

questions.q: The Optimized Link State Routing (OLSR) protocol uses _____ to optimize transmission.
questions.exp: OLSR uses Multipoint Relays (MPRs) — a selected subset of neighbours — to forward control messages, reducing redundant broadcasts.

questions.q: Optimized Link State Routing Protocol uses triggered updates of its routing table whenever there is a topology change.
questions.exp: False — OLSR is a proactive protocol that uses periodic updates, not triggered updates. Reactive protocols like DSR use triggered (on-demand) route discovery.

questions.q: Which of the following are NOT proactive mesh routing algorithms?
opts: Better Approach To Mobile Adhoc Networking
questions.exp: AODV, DSR, and TORA are reactive (on-demand) protocols. BATMAN, DSDV, WRP, and OLSR are proactive (table-driven) protocols.

questions.q: Which of the following are NOT reactive mesh routing algorithms?
opts: Better Approach To Mobile Adhoc Networking
questions.exp: BATMAN, OLSR, WRP, and DSDV are proactive protocols (not reactive). DSR, TORA, and AODV are the reactive (on-demand) ones.
